speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 13, 1996. Rescuer, Johtje Vos, maiden name, Kuyper. Interviewer, Naomi Rappaport. City is Monticello. The state, New York. The country, USA. Language, English. My name is Naomi Rappaport. N-A-O-M-I R-A-P-P-A-P-O-R-T. And the date is August 13, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Johtje Vos Kuyper in Monticello, New York, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon, Mrs. Vos. And thank you very much for joining us at the Shoah Foundation and telling us your experiences during World War II as a rescuer. We'd like to start with your name and could you spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- the name is Johtje Vos. And you spell it J-O-H-T-J-E, which is a short name for Johanna, which is my official name. But nobody [INAUDIBLE] calls me so. And Vos is the last name, V-O-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Johanna, even though it's not used?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-H-A-N-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had a second name was Hendrika. But I threw that out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How do you spell Hendrika?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-N-D-R-I-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? Was it different than--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Johanna Hendrika Kuyper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kuyper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-Y-P-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Nono.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Nono?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-O-N-O. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was because my older sister couldn't say Johtje and she said [DUTCH] And I myself said, no, no. And [DUTCH] means little nut. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is December 29, 1909.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amersfoort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-M-E-R-S-F-O-O-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much. You grew up in Amersfoort-- you were born in Amersfoort.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Amersfoort.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family, from what you told me, was in different places, moved around in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father was a career officer, so he was moved once in awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Amersfoort?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which of your homes do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next one was Breda and that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Where is Breda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breda is in the south of Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I remember an accident. There were horses who were-- had lost control. Two horses. And I was playing in the garden and the horses came straight to our garden. And my mother yelled at me, and said, stand completely still. Don't move. Don't move. I don't know why that was the best thing to do, but anyway. They didn't hurt me. But that I-- it's one of my earliest remembrances.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was Breda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was Breda?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Middle, middle-size place. It was an-- garrison-- military place.

speaker: interviewer
text: A military garrison. And who lived there? What kind of people lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Dutch people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. When I ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not an--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind, I mean, what kind of work did they do? Was-- what was the community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was too young. I don't remember much of it. I know my father was a-- was the major of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was the mayor of the town as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a major.

speaker: interviewer
text: Major.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And do you remember your house there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I do remember the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a typical stone Dutch house with a garden in front and a garden in the back and a little iron fence. They're all memories I have of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your own house, or did it belong to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know. No. I think it was a rented house. Because my father was moved from one town to the other as an officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in which community did you spend most of your time-- your growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leiden. L-E-I-D-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Leiden.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a university town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. How old were you when you got to Leiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: [WHISTLES] You ask difficult questions. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately. Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'd think six or seven. Because I was in elementary school in Leiden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the whole family together in Leiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your father was still in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was commander of Leiden, of that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does commander of Leiden mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Commander means that he was the highest officer there. And it was in a military town. I mean, there was a big group of military people, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much politics in the-- when one becomes a commander in a town, a military commander?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was very involved in politic-- with-- because his father was prime minister of Holland. And that was, of course, a very political position.

speaker: interviewer
text: His father's name was what--

speaker: interviewee
text: His father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Kuyper.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: His father's name was Kuyper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Could you spell his first name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham. A--

speaker: interviewer
text: Abraham? Abraham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was the prime minister--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prime minister of Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And how did that affect you growing up? Did you meet lots of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --political figure--

speaker: interviewee
text: --during the first world war, my father was at the frontier and-- of the-- of the country. And my mother and my two sisters and I lived with my grandfather. So I have a very lively memory of all that. And it was interesting because, you know, Prince Hendrik would come and visit and, you know, and things like that, so-- I sat on his knees one day. So it was-- so, yeah. It was an interesting time for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the political climate growing up in-- under a prime minister and a major?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, our political party, which was founded by my grandfather, was anti-revolutionary. A

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's still an-- party, large party in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what anti-revolutionary means in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I can't. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it against? What revolution?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't know. I'm-- I'm totally uninterested in-- in politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the party in power, the anti-revolutionary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And still. It's still in power.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a democratic party? A--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an-- a Christian party.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, the church had involvement in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there or was there, when you were growing up, a division between church and state? And when-- were-- were the schools run by the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were schools that were Christian schools. And there was a Christian high school, and a Christian University, which was founded by my grandfather. And, yeah, we went, of course, to Christian schools, which meant that we had an-- we all came-- came to all our-- all the classes together and they read something from the Bible and we prayed. And then we went all to our classrooms and we didn't hear about religion anymore. But that was the-- the spirit of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was it a Protestant--

speaker: interviewee
text: Protestant. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which branch of Protestantism was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Christian reformed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the same thing as Dutch reformed, or is there a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dutch reformed, Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dutch reform.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does this still exist? This party-- this church and this division?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's still in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's still.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is the government still--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --run--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It isn't. I see. Were-- what happened to those children, or those people, who did not choose to go to Christian reform schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were public, public schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were public schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. More public schools than Christian schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any contact with those children in Leiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. We lived in a dead end lane and-- very [INAUDIBLE] lane in Leiden. And we had a young people's club there. And there were all the Catholic children and they were all [INAUDIBLE], you know? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- you said you were in Leiden for how long? About?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left home when I-- when my parents were still in Leiden. And I was, I think, 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your growing up period--

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Leiden, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was in-- in Leiden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the ethnic distribution of-- were there-- was there ethnic diversity in Leiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what-- who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were the intellectual people and the professional people, and the people who did other work. And Holland was quite conscious about differences in that time. It's not so now anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up in Leiden, were you-- did you have any contact with the Jewish community there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not a special Jewish community. There were Jewish people living everywhere. But the Jewish community, I've never heard of in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you know Jews in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you friendly with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a Jewish boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. In-- in Leiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leiden.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Leiden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And having a Jewish boyfriend-- was that an unusual thing? Or something that other people had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- in our religion, it was an unusual thing. And it was-- it didn't please my parents. Not because he was Jewish, but he was not a believer in God. If he had-- my sister married a Jewish man, and-- but he was a believer. And so my parents found that perfectly all right. But my boyfriend was an atheist. And of course, that was not accepted in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you resolve that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Paris. We-- We just-- he was a doctor. And when-- I was a student-- medical student. And when he made his-- was ready with his-- with his study, he went to Indonesia, on purpose, so that we could-- and I went to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we both went to completely different atmosphere. Because he couldn't marry me, because he was an-- an-- an-- how you call that-- he-- he couldn't-- his children couldn't be Jewish when he married to Christian girl. That was the reason. And when I lived for a year in Paris, he came and said, you are more dear to me than-- (LAUGHS) than-- than the religion. And so he make up his mind that he want to marry me. But he was too late. (LAUGHS) I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with other Jews growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way? Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- well, I don't even know who was Jewish and who was not Jewish. That-- That was not the point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit in Jewish homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit in Jewish homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit in Jewish homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware of customs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and ways of life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I liked-- I liked certain festivities and to celebrate with them. That-- the day which is around Easter-- what's that name again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Yeah. The-- was there much anti-Semitism in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Leiden, that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I knew, there was-- there was no anti-Semitism in Holland. As far as I know. I've never noticed or anything. Never heard of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in--

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Jewish people in Germany would all flee, when Hitler came, to Holland, of course, because they felt safe there. And they were safe there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst your relatives and your family, your-- your grandfather, your parents-- were they-- did they influence your attitudes towards Jews or other ethnic groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I can't say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it ever discussed, the fact-- how did they handle the fact that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you had a Jewish boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they wanted-- they didn't want to-- me to marry my Jewish boyfriend, not because he was Jewish, but because he was not a believer in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the first question my father asked of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they handle your sister's marriage to a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he became a believer.

speaker: interviewer
text: A believer in God, or did he convert?

speaker: interviewee
text: He converted.

speaker: interviewer
text: He converted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he became a Christian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he fully accepted into the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Totally accepted, yeah. But they lived not in the same country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in-- in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was a doctor and she had her practice in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Paris and married in Paris, am I correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was 19 years-- 20 about. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was-- it was before the rise of Nazism--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no. Hitler was already there and my first husband-- I'm-- I've been married twice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and my first husband left Holland. And he was an artist. And he went to Paris, not only because he was an artist, but also because he didn't want to live in Germany with Hitler. He was not Jewish. But he was just very anti-Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but he-- you were living in Germany as well, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never lived in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were living in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- and then you moved to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- no. We married in Paris and lived in Paris. And then when we had-- started to have children, we went back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What [SIGHS] was the reaction in your family-- your husband, your parents-- to the rise of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard all about it. Because, you know, Holland and Germany are practically one country. [LAUGHS] And so everybody I knew was incredibly upset and-- and-- and miserable about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Even before we were occupied ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Nazi party in Leiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- your parents were still in Leiden at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Nazi party in--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Nazi party, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who it belonged to it? What kind of people belonged to the Nazi party?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dutch people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Dutch people. Yeah. There was a Dutch Nazi party.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had our own Hitler. We had [NON-ENGLISH] Mussert, who was the head of the-- of the National Socialistic Party.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mussert. M-U-S-S-E-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mussert, [INAUDIBLE]. Was there any impact on the Jewish community in your town-- in Leiden-- by the Dutch Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not a Jewish community. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, the-- the Jews who were living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish who lived there, well, they all were scared of Mussert, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any violence when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we hid them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We hid the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when did you-- that was later, was it not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was as soon as Hitler started to enter-- in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But prior to Hitler's invasion of Holland--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Prior to that, we didn't have to protect anybody, with nothing happened to them yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there-- were there speeches, and meetings, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --stuff of that sort? And were any Jews hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: But Mussert started a little after Hitler in-- in-- had started in Germany, of course. And then when Germany came to-- how you say-- occupied Holland, then Mussert was there. But he was under the thumb of Hitler. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1939 and earlier, '38, with Kristallnacht--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when there were all these laws that went into effect in Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jews began to migrate to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any Jewish refugees who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do, and how did you relate to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they found jobs and they were-- people were very nice to them. They welcomed them. I mean, they didn't ask them to come, but when they came and had to flee-- there were, of course-- there were also people in Holland who resented it. But the majority of the Dutch people were very anti-Hitler-- the vast majority-- and so the helped the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those who resented it, how did they show their resentment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they show their resentment, those who resented the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I couldn't-- they talked about it, and the wrote in the paper about it, you know, and things like that. But for-- they didn't attack them, or they didn't do anything to them. But they were just resentful, they didn't like it that so many Jewish people came to Holland. But they couldn't do anything about it. Because in general, the Dutch people agreed fully to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- do you remember the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I don't remember that well. That was in 1940. Then I was-- yeah, then I was married, and-- yeah, I remember that a lot of-- I-- I remember that first of all, a lot of Jewish people came to Holland, and that they had a terrible shock when the Germans invaded our country. And they-- most of them-- many of them tried to flee to England. Certainly, and [LAUGHS] everybody who had a boat, or could get near a boat, went. Because that was not such a far trip, not even to England. And the ones who were-- stayed in Holland, started to hide. So we had a lot of Jewish people in our house. By that time I was married. I was married to my second husband. But I still had-- my first husband was German. And so in Holland, when a young girl in Holland-- or a woman in Holland-- marries a foreigner, she loses her-- in that time-- her nationality. So I became a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And even when I was divorced, and married my Dutch husband, I still was German. Because I could not apply, because it was in the war that I married. And I couldn't apply because I couldn't attract any attention to us, because we had so many Jewish people hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a-- you were a German citizen, or-- or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, German citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: German citizen. How-- were you considered an enemy alien? Or how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all my friends and everybody I knew, my family, knew that I was not an alien, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And other people didn't know, I mean, I didn't tell it around, I am German.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when the-- the Germans did come in, did they have expectations? Did they know you were German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because I had a-- for instance, we had distribution cards. And I got double distribution cards, because I was German. And it was wonderful, because I could help the Jewish people with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they expect you to do other things?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't get the-- they didn't get the distribution cards. So when I got double ones for myself and my two, then, German daughters, I could use them to help the Jewish people who were hidden in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans who had invaded in May, and-- and then, of course, Holland surrendered in May 14, did the Germans have expectations of you, as a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to be a Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't know that I was German. I mean, and I didn't give myself up to any group, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had asked earlier if you remember when the Germans came in. When did you see your first Germans in-- you were in Leiden, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Leiden, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Leiden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see your first Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they occupy Leiden heavily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He occupied every--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every town or village and city was occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they live, and what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they confiscated places-- for instance, many Jewish houses, from Jews who had fled, were taken by the Germans. And then they had the [NON-ENGLISH], and, you know, and, you know, bridges.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a visible army there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We saw them all the time, yeah. We hated those green uniforms. [LAUGHS] Walking through our Dutch villages and see these.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware, or when did you become aware, of the laws against the Jews from the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it came in the papers. We read about it, and, you know-- very shortly after the Germans had-- had occupied Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what laws were visible?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- there were German papers, certainly, in Holland, German newspapers. And we didn't want to read them. So then they put it also in the Dutch papers. And so we knew all about, yeah. There was nothing secret about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of those laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of those laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember. That we all had to register ourselves. And, of course, we were not allowed to have Jewish people in our house-- visiting, or living, or whatever. And things like that, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- could you recognize a Jew immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not every Jew looks Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't mean by-- I meant by clothing, or by--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they had to wear the star. You know. They had that yellow, big star. But when that was an order, that we-- that all the Jewish people had to wear that star, we took stars, too, and wore them, to mix the Germans up. But by that time, we had a lot of Jewish people in our house, already, hidden. And they begged my husband and me not to wear that star, because they said you'd attract attention to yourself with that. So we didn't do it. We took it off again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you personally-- did you personally see any violence against Jews at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have never seen it myself. But my husband went off into Amsterdam. We lived in the country. And when my husband went to Amsterdam, he always came home all shook up, and pale and miserable from what he had seen there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what had he seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had seen Jewish people being thrown in-- in open animal carts from the train, you know, and-- you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do? What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was an agriculturist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you lived, could he practice agriculture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He-- he-- what did he actually do in the war? He did nothing but trying to find food for his family. And afterwards, he-- well, after the war, we came to America. And here, we had a children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life, your own life, change when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for the first time, I had fear. And-- and we started immediately to be very active to help the Jewish people. So that-- that included, also, a lot of fear, of course, and danger. So our life-- our life changed very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What decided you to help the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were in need of help. I mean, it's like when you see a child fall in the water, you jump in and get it out. I wouldn't, because I can't swim. [LAUGHS] But-- but every other decent people will do it. And that's the same thing. We had Jewish friends and. We heard about all that-- all the laws that came out to the Jews, so we said we'll help them. So it started out with the child. The Jewish people in our village, or in the villages, had-- were forced to come to a Jewish part in-- in Amsterdam. And when those friends of ours, who were very famous musicians, when they were ordered to go to Amsterdam, they had a little boy. And they were extremely worried to take the child there, because they didn't know what was going to happen. So we said, well, give him to us. And we took him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nap-- N-A-P-- that was his first name-- De-- D-E-- Klyn-- K-L-Y-N. And he was a very famous violinist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife was also a musician?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She was a pianist, and her name was Alice. A-L-I-S-E. C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the child's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they stayed-- they left the child with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left the child. And they also were in hiding, and when there was a difficulty, or a danger, then they came to us. But we lived in the same village where they lived. And so it was too dangerous to have them with us-- for them. Because people who would come to us might recognize them. So they-- were not hidden-- they once in awhile, for a week or so, when there was trouble, they would come to us. But in general, they were hidden in Amsterdam with other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- did-- did people know you were hiding people-- Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which people, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Leiden, your friends, or-- who knew that you were [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people who came to our house. And we became very, very careful with who came to our house. But of course, they had a room upstairs. And they-- and-- so, sometimes we had people who we didn't know-- want to know that we had Jewish people in the house. But our friends and all knew it. And they had did this themselves, the same as we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children knew that there were Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: My children knew, of course, that they were living with us, but they didn't know they were Jewish. I mean, we never talked about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. I'm going to change the tape right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.
